Demonstrates how to embed an image and formatted text into an existing PDF using the PdfFileMend class.
rules: Instantiate PdfFileMend with a Document: var mendor = new PdfFileMend({doc});
Add an image to a page: mendor.AddImage({image_stream}, {page}, {int}, {int}, {int}, {int}); // x, y, width, height

Create a FormattedText object and add it to a page: var ft = new FormattedText({string_literal}, {color}, {font}, {encoding}, {bool}, {float}); mendor.AddText(ft, {page}, {int}, {int}, {int}, {int}); // x, y, width, height
Finalize changes by calling mendor.Close();
warnings: The example creates an output FileStream but never writes the modified PDF to {output_pdf}; PdfFileMend operates on the in-memory Document, so an explicit save may be required.
The FormattedText constructor uses FontStyle and EncodingType from the Facades namespace; ensure correct using directives.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to export selected annotation types from a PDF document to an XFDF file using the PdfAnnotationEditor facade.
rules: Instantiate {class:PdfAnnotationEditor}, bind the source PDF with BindPdf({input_pdf}), then call ExportAnnotationsXfdf({output_stream}, {int:startPage}, {int:endPage}, {string_literal[]:annotationTypes}) to write annotations to XFDF.
Specify desired annotation types as string values obtained from AnnotationType enum (e.g., AnnotationType.Text.ToString()) and pass them in the string array argument of ExportAnnotationsXfdf.
The page range parameters for ExportAnnotationsXfdf are 1‑based inclusive indices; ensure they fall within the document's page count.
warnings: The example does not explicitly dispose the FileStream; callers should ensure proper disposal.
ExportAnnotationsXfdf may only support a subset of annotation types; verify compatibility for other types.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to import specific annotation types from an XFDF file into an existing PDF using the PdfAnnotationEditor facade.
rules: Create a PdfAnnotationEditor, bind it to {input_pdf} with BindPdf, then call ImportAnnotationFromXfdf passing a readable stream of {xfdf_file} and an array of {annotation_types} to import only those annotation kinds, finally save to {output_pdf}.
When importing, supply an AnnotationType[] containing the desired enum values (e.g., AnnotationType.Text) to filter which annotations are imported from the XFDF source.
warnings: The PdfAnnotationEditor class resides in the Aspose.Pdf.Facades namespace, which may be deprecated in newer Aspose.PDF releases; consider using the Document class with XfdfLoadOptions if available.
The example assumes the XFDF file matches the structure of {input_pdf}; mismatched references may cause missing or misplaced annotations.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to change the page size of selected pages in an existing PDF using PdfPageEditor and how to query the assigned page size.
rules: To resize specific pages: create a PdfPageEditor, bind {input_pdf} via BindPdf, set ProcessPages = new int[] { {int} } for the target pages, assign the desired size using PageSize = PageSize.{string_literal}, then call Save({output_pdf}).
To retrieve the current size of a page: after BindPdf({input_pdf}), call GetPageSize({int}) which returns a PageSize enum value.
warnings: PageSize enum only supports predefined sizes; custom dimensions require a different API.
ProcessPages expects 1‑based page numbers; providing out‑of‑range indices will cause runtime errors.

semantic_summary: Shows how to add a text field to an existing PDF form, set its required attribute and limit the number of characters, then save the modified document using the FormEditor API.
rules: Instantiate a {Document} with the path to {input_pdf} and pass it to a new {FormEditor} instance.
Add a form field using {FormEditor}.AddField(FieldType.{field_type}, {string_literal}, {int page}, {int x1}, {int y1}, {int x2}, {int y2}) to define its type, name and rectangular position.
Set visual or behavioral attributes with {FormEditor}.SetFieldAttribute({string_literal}, PropertyFlag.{attribute}) (e.g., Required).
Restrict the maximum characters accepted by the field using {FormEditor}.SetFieldLimit({string_literal}, {int maxLength}).
Persist the changes by calling {FormEditor}.Save({output_pdf}).
warnings: Coordinate values are specified in points with the origin at the bottom‑left of the page; ensure correct conversion if using other units.
Page numbers are 1‑based; providing a page number outside the document range will raise an exception.

semantic_summary: Shows how to merge two PDF documents in memory by loading them into MemoryStream objects and using Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfFileEditor.Concatenate to produce a combined PDF.
rules: Instantiate a PdfFileEditor and call Concatenate({input_pdf_stream1}, {input_pdf_stream2}, {output_pdf_stream}) to merge PDFs provided as streams.
When PDFs are available as byte arrays, wrap each byte array in a MemoryStream ({memory_stream}) before passing to PdfFileEditor.
After concatenation, obtain the merged PDF bytes via {output_pdf_stream}.ToArray() and write them to a file or further processing.
warnings: The example manually reads files into byte arrays; using File.ReadAllBytes would be simpler.
Output FileStream is not wrapped in a using statement, which may lead to resource leaks.
Ensure input MemoryStreams are positioned at the beginning before calling Concatenate.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to concatenate multiple PDFs, insert a blank page for a Table of Contents, add text stamps as headings and links, and create local navigation links using Aspose.PDF for .NET.
rules: To concatenate PDFs, instantiate {type:PdfFileEditor} and call Concatenate({input_pdf1_stream}, {input_pdf2_stream}, {output_pdf_stream}); each stream must be opened in appropriate FileMode.
To insert a blank page at a specific position, load the resulting PDF into {type:Document} and invoke Pages.Insert({int pageNumber}); the inserted page is empty and can host further content.

To add a text stamp (e.g., a TOC heading) to a page, create a {type:Stamp}, bind a {type:FormattedText} with desired {string_literal}, {color}, {font}, {int fontSize}, set its origin via SetOrigin({float x}, {float y})—optionally using PdfFileInfo.GetPageWidth({doc}, {int page}) for dynamic positioning—assign Stamp.Pages = new int[] { {int targetPage} }, then add it with {type:PdfFileStamp}.AddStamp(stamp).

To create a clickable local link, bind the PDF with {type:PdfContentEditor}.BindPdf({doc}) and call CreateLocalLink(new Rectangle({int x}, {int y}, {int width}, {int height}), {int targetPage}, {int page}, {color linkColor}); the rectangle defines the clickable area on {int page} that navigates to {int targetPage}.
warnings: PdfFileInfo.GetPageWidth overload used with a FileStream may vary between library versions; placeholder assumes method signature GetPageWidth({doc}, {int page}).
EncodingType.Winansi is used; ensure the enum value exists in the target Aspose.PDF version.

semantic_summary: Shows how to merge two PDF form files into a single PDF while ensuring that form field names remain unique by configuring the PdfFileEditor.
rules: When concatenating PDFs that contain form fields, set PdfFileEditor.KeepFieldsUnique = true and define PdfFileEditor.UniqueSuffix (e.g., "_{NUM}") to automatically rename duplicate fields.
Invoke PdfFileEditor.Concatenate({input_pdf}, {input_pdf}, {output_pdf}) to merge the specified source PDFs into the target PDF.
warnings: UniqueSuffix uses the placeholder %NUM% which is replaced with an incremental number; ensure the pattern does not conflict with existing field names.

semantic_summary: Shows how to merge every PDF file in a specified folder into a single PDF document using Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfFileEditor.Concatenate.
rules: Collect all source PDFs with Directory.GetFiles({doc}, "*.pdf") and pass the resulting string[] as {input_pdf} to PdfFileEditor.Concatenate({input_pdf}, {output_pdf}).
Create a unique {output_pdf} name by combining DateTime.Now formatted strings (e.g., "MM-dd-yyyy" and "hh-mm") with a suffix like "_out.pdf".
warnings: The Concatenate method overwrites the target file if it already exists; ensure the generated {output_pdf} name is unique or handle existing files accordingly.

semantic_summary: Shows how to convert a PDF/FDF file to an XML representation by streaming the source file into Aspose.Pdf.Facades.FormDataConverter.
rules: Open the source PDF/FDF as a {input_pdf} FileStream (FileMode.Open) and the destination XML as a {output_xml} FileStream (FileMode.Create), then invoke FormDataConverter.ConvertFdfToXml({input_pdf}, {output_xml}).
The conversion method works directly with streams; no Aspose.Pdf.Document instance is required.
warnings: The example passes a .pdf file to ConvertFdfToXml, which semantically expects an FDF stream; behavior may depend on file content.
Streams are not disposed; in production code wrap them in using statements.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to create hierarchical (nested) bookmarks in an existing PDF using Aspose.Pdf.Facades by building Bookmark objects, nesting them via Bookmarks collections, and saving the modified document.
rules: Bind an existing PDF file ({input_pdf}) to a PdfContentEditor instance before manipulating its bookmark structure.
Create a Bookmark, set its Action to "GoTo", assign a target page number ({int}) via PageNumber, and give it a title ({string_literal}).
To build a hierarchy, place child Bookmark objects into a Bookmarks collection and assign that collection to the parent Bookmark's ChildItems property.
Add top‑level Bookmark objects to a separate Bookmarks collection if multiple root chapters are needed, then assign each collection to the corresponding parent Bookmark.
After constructing the full bookmark tree, call PdfContentEditor.Save({output_pdf}) to persist the changes.
warnings: The example uses the literal string "GoTo" for the Action property; future versions may provide an enum or constant.
Bookmarks must be added to a Bookmarks collection before being assigned to ChildItems; directly adding a Bookmark to ChildItems is not shown.
The code assumes the input PDF already exists at the specified path; no validation is performed.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to create a PDF portfolio (collection) using Aspose.PDF, embed external files such as Excel, Word, and images with descriptions, and save the resulting PDF.
rules: To build a PDF portfolio, instantiate a {doc} of type Aspose.Pdf.Document, assign a new Aspose.Pdf.Collection to its Collection property, add one or more FileSpecification objects to {doc}.Collection, then call {doc}.Save({string_literal}).
To embed an external file, create a FileSpecification with the file path, optionally set its Description property, and add it to the document's Collection via {doc}.Collection.Add({file_spec}).
warnings: The example relies on a helper method RunExamples.GetDataDir_AsposePdf_TechnicalArticles() for the data directory, which is not shown.
File paths are assumed to be valid and the referenced files must exist at runtime.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to read existing custom PDF attributes via the Header collection and how to add or retrieve custom metadata using PdfFileInfo's SetMetaInfo and GetMetaInfo methods.
rules: Instantiate {pdf_file_info} = new Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfFileInfo({input_pdf}) to access PDF meta information.
Read all existing custom attributes by enumerating {pdf_file_info}.Header, which returns an IDictionary of key/value pairs.
Add or update a custom attribute using {pdf_file_info}.SetMetaInfo({string_literal}, {string_literal}).
Retrieve a specific custom attribute value with {pdf_file_info}.GetMetaInfo({string_literal}).
warnings: SetMetaInfo modifies the in‑memory PDF metadata; changes are persisted only after saving the PDF (not shown in the example).
Header may not reflect attributes added via SetMetaInfo until the document is saved and reloaded.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to extract form field names and their bounding rectangles from one PDF and then add new text fields at the same locations in another PDF using Aspose.PDF facades.
rules: Open {input_pdf} with Aspose.Pdf.Facades.Form, retrieve all field names via .FieldNames, and for each name call GetFieldFacade(name).Box to obtain a {rect} representing the field location.

Load a target PDF into Aspose.Pdf.Document, create a FormEditor for that document, and add a new field with FormEditor.AddField(FieldType.Text, {string_literal}, {string_literal}, {int page}, {float left}, {float top}, {float right}, {float bottom}) using the previously captured rectangle coordinates.
Persist changes by calling Save on either Form or FormEditor, specifying {output_pdf} as the destination path.
warnings: The example uses System.Drawing.Rectangle for coordinates; conversion may be needed for other coordinate systems.
All added fields are placed on page 1; adjust the page number placeholder if fields reside on different pages.

semantic_summary: Shows how to edit various page-level properties such as alignment, transition, size, rotation, zoom, display duration and origin offset of a PDF using Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfPageEditor.
rules: Instantiate {class:PdfPageEditor}, call BindPdf({input_pdf}), set ProcessPages to an int[] of target {page} numbers, then assign desired page attributes (HorizontalAlignment, TransitionType, TransitionDuration, PageSize, Rotation, Zoom, DisplayDuration) before invoking Save({output_pdf}).
Retrieve current page settings after modification using GetPageRotation({page}) and GetPageSize({page}) on the same PdfPageEditor instance.
Adjust the coordinate origin of the edited pages with MovePosition({int}, {int}) to shift the (0,0) point before saving.
warnings: Enum types HorizontalAlignment and PageSize are assumed to be in Aspose.Pdf.Facades namespace; if they reside elsewhere adjust the fully qualified names.
TransitionType is set using an integer value; the specific enum (if any) is not identified in the sample.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to bind an existing PDF, add custom XMP metadata properties using Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfXmpMetadata, and save the enriched document.
rules: Bind an input PDF stream to a PdfXmpMetadata instance: xmpMetaData.BindPdf({input_pdf});
Add XMP metadata entries using keys from DefaultMetadataProperties: xmpMetaData.Add({metadata_key}, {string_literal});
Persist the updated PDF with XMP metadata to an output stream: xmpMetaData.Save({output_pdf});
Close any opened streams after the operation to release resources.
warnings: The exact type (enum or static class) of DefaultMetadataProperties is inferred; if it differs, adjust the rule accordingly.
The example uses FileStream directly; callers must ensure proper disposal (e.g., using statements) in production code.

semantic_summary: Shows how to load a PDF portfolio, enumerate its embedded files, read each file's content stream, and save the extracted files to disk.
rules: Load a PDF portfolio into a Document object ({doc} = new Document({input_pdf})) and retrieve its EmbeddedFiles collection ({embeddedFiles} = {doc}.EmbeddedFiles).

Iterate each FileSpecification ({fileSpec}) in the EmbeddedFiles collection, read its Contents stream into a byte array, and write the bytes to a file (e.g., using FileStream) with a filename derived from Path.GetFileName({fileSpec}.Name).

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to use Aspose.Pdf.Facades.FormEditor to create, modify, and manage PDF form fields such as text boxes, list boxes, and submit buttons, including setting attributes, moving, renaming, and saving the updated document.
rules: Create a FormEditor instance by passing a loaded {doc} to the constructor.

Add a form field with editor.AddField(FieldType.{field_type}, {field_name}, {page}, {int}, {int}, {int}, {int}) where the last four integers define the lower‑left and upper‑right coordinates of the field rectangle.
For a ListBox field, populate items using editor.AddListItem({field_name}, {string_literal}) and optionally remove items with editor.DelListItem({field_name}, {string_literal}).

Add a submit button using editor.AddSubmitBtn({field_name}, {page}, {string_literal}, {string_literal}, {int}, {int}, {int}, {int}) where the second string is the button label and the third string is the target URL.
Modify field geometry with editor.MoveField({field_name}, {int}, {int}, {int}, {int}) to reposition and resize the field.
Rename, remove, or reset fields using editor.RenameField({old_name}, {new_name}), editor.RemoveField({field_name}), and editor.ResetFacade() respectively.

Configure visual and behavioral attributes: editor.SetFieldAlignment({field_name}, FormFieldFacade.Align{Left|Center|Right}), editor.SetFieldAppearance({field_name}, AnnotationFlags.{NoRotate|...}), editor.SetFieldAttribute({field_name}, PropertyFlag.{ReadOnly|Required|...}), and editor.SetFieldLimit({field_name}, {int}) to set a maximum character length.
Persist changes with editor.Save({output_pdf}).
warnings: The exact namespace of the Document class is inferred as Aspose.Pdf.Document; the source imports Aspose.Pdf.Facades but does not import Aspose.Pdf directly.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to enumerate existing form fields, obtain their on‑page coordinates, and programmatically add new text fields positioned relative to those coordinates using Aspose.PDF Facades.
rules: Use {input_pdf} with Aspose.Pdf.Facades.Form to access form fields via the FieldNames property.
For each field name, call Form.GetFieldFacade({field_name}).Box to retrieve its {rect} (location on the page).
Instantiate Aspose.Pdf.Document with a target PDF and create a FormEditor({doc}) to modify form structures.

Add a new field with FormEditor.AddField(FieldType.{field_type}, {string_literal}, {existing_field_name}, {int page}, {float left}, {float top}, {float right}, {float bottom}) to place it relative to an existing field's coordinates.
Save the modified document using Form.Save({output_pdf}) or FormEditor.Save({output_pdf}) after changes.
warnings: Coordinates returned by FormFieldFacade.Box are in points; ensure they match the coordinate system expected by FormEditor.AddField.
The example uses two different source PDFs; the field names and coordinates are taken from the first PDF but added to the second, which may not be appropriate in all scenarios.

semantic_summary: Shows how to fill a text form field in a PDF and apply justified alignment to the field using the Form and FormEditor facades.
rules: Bind a PDF {input_pdf} with Form.BindPdf, fill a form field using Form.FillField({field_name}, {string_literal}), save the modified PDF to a stream with Form.Save, then bind that stream to FormEditor.

Set FormEditor.Facade.Alignment = FormFieldFacade.AlignJustified before calling FormEditor.DecorateField() to apply justified alignment to the targeted form field, and finally persist the result with FormEditor.Save({output_pdf}).
warnings: The example assumes the field "Text1" exists and is a text field; AlignJustified is only applicable to text form fields.
Streams must be rewound (Seek) before binding to FormEditor; forgetting this may cause empty output.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to rotate specific pages of a PDF document and how to query the rotation angle of a page using Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfPageEditor.
rules: To rotate selected pages in {input_pdf}, instantiate PdfPageEditor, call BindPdf({input_pdf}), set ProcessPages to an array of {page} numbers, assign Rotation to {int} (allowed values: 0, 90, 180, 270), then invoke Save({output_pdf}).
To obtain the rotation angle of a specific {page} in {input_pdf}, bind the PDF with PdfPageEditor and call GetPageRotation({page}), which returns the rotation in {int} degrees.
warnings: Rotation must be a multiple of 90 degrees; other values may be ignored or cause errors.
ProcessPages uses 1‑based indexing; ensure page numbers correspond to the PDF's page order.

semantic_summary: Shows how to use Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfAnnotationEditor to bind a PDF, modify, delete, extract, import annotations and save the resulting document.
rules: Bind a PDF file to a PdfAnnotationEditor instance before performing any annotation operations: editor.BindPdf({input_pdf});

Create a concrete annotation (e.g., FreeTextAnnotation) with a target page, rectangle, and default appearance, then set its properties (e.g., Subject) before applying: new FreeTextAnnotation(editor.Document.Pages[{page}], {rect}, new DefaultAppearance({font}, {int}, {color}));
Modify annotations on a page range by calling ModifyAnnotations({int pageStart}, {int pageEnd}, {annotation});
Delete all annotations of a given type using DeleteAnnotation({string_literal annotationType});

Extract annotations of specific types from a page range with ExtractAnnotations({int pageStart}, {int pageEnd}, string[] {annotationTypes}) which returns an ArrayList of Annotation objects; iterate and access properties like Contents.
Import annotations from one or more external PDFs using ImportAnnotations(string[] {sourcePdfPaths});
Persist changes by saving the edited document: editor.Save({output_pdf});
warnings: The example uses non‑generic ArrayList; casting is required to work with Annotation objects.
Page numbers are 1‑based; ensure correct indexing when accessing editor.Document.Pages.
DeleteAnnotation expects the exact annotation type name as a string (e.g., "Stamp"); mismatched case may cause no deletions.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to use Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfExtractor to determine whether a PDF contains text, images, both, or neither.
rules: Bind the PDF file to a PdfExtractor with BindPdf({input_pdf}), call ExtractText(), then retrieve the extracted text into a stream via GetText({stream}); if {stream}.Length >= 1 the document contains text.
After binding the same PDF, call ExtractImage() and then evaluate HasNextImage(); if true the document contains at least one image.
warnings: GetText writes the extracted text to a stream; using a MemoryStream and checking Length is one way to detect text presence, but other overloads (e.g., GetText(string)) exist.
HasNextImage only indicates the presence of a first image; to enumerate all images you would need a while loop with HasNextImage() and GetNextImage().

semantic_summary: Shows how to use Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfExtractor to extract text, images, and attachments from a PDF, optionally per page, and save the extracted content to files.
rules: Set {doc}.Password to the PDF password (or an empty string) before calling BindPdf.
Define the extraction range by assigning {int} values to {doc}.StartPage and {doc}.EndPage prior to extraction.
Bind the source PDF using {doc}.BindPdf({input_pdf}) before invoking any extraction methods.
To extract all text, call {doc}.ExtractText() then {doc}.GetText({output_pdf}); for per‑page text, loop while {doc}.HasNextPageText() and invoke {doc}.GetNextPageText({output_pdf}) for each page.
To extract images, call {doc}.ExtractImage() then iterate while {doc}.HasNextImage() and use {doc}.GetNextImage({output_pdf}, {string_literal}) to save each image.
To extract embedded files, call {doc}.ExtractAttachment() followed by {doc}.GetAttachment({output_folder}) to write all attachments to the specified directory.
warnings: GetNextImage requires a System.Drawing.Imaging.ImageFormat argument, which may need an additional assembly reference.
HasNextPageText/GetNextPageText only return results after ExtractText has been called.
GetAttachment extracts all attachments to the provided folder; individual attachment selection is not demonstrated.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates various PDF manipulation operations using Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfFileEditor, including appending, concatenating, deleting, extracting, inserting pages, creating booklets, N‑up layouts, and splitting PDFs into pages or custom groups.
rules: Use PdfFileEditor.Append({input_pdf}, {port_pdf}, {int start}, {int end}, {output_pdf}) to copy a page range from a source PDF into another PDF at the end of the destination.
Use PdfFileEditor.Concatenate({input_pdf1}, {input_pdf2}, {output_pdf}) to merge two PDFs sequentially into a single document.
Use PdfFileEditor.Delete({input_pdf}, {int[] pages}, {output_pdf}) to remove specific pages identified by their numbers from a PDF.
Set PdfFileEditor.OwnerPassword = {string_literal} before calling Extract({input_pdf}, {int start}, {int end}, {output_pdf}) to extract a page range from a password‑protected PDF.
Use PdfFileEditor.Insert({input_pdf}, {int position}, {port_pdf}, {int start}, {int end}, {output_pdf}) to insert a page range from a secondary PDF into a primary PDF at a specified page index.
Use PdfFileEditor.MakeBooklet({input_pdf}, {output_pdf}) to reorder pages for booklet printing.
Use PdfFileEditor.MakeNUp({input_pdf}, {output_pdf}, {int rows}, {int columns}) to create an N‑up layout with the given rows and columns per sheet.
Use PdfFileEditor.SplitFromFirst({input_pdf}, {int pageCount}, {output_pdf}) to split the first {pageCount} pages into a separate file.
Use PdfFileEditor.SplitToPages({input_pdf}) to obtain an array of MemoryStream, each containing a single page, which can be written to individual files.
Use PdfFileEditor.SplitToBulks({input_pdf}, {int[][] pageGroups}) to split the PDF into multiple documents where each document contains the pages defined in the corresponding sub‑array.
warnings: The example sets OwnerPassword directly on the PdfFileEditor instance; this property must be configured before calling Extract on encrypted PDFs.
MemoryStream objects returned by SplitToPages and SplitToBulks should be disposed after use to avoid resource leaks.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to digitally sign PDF documents using Aspose.PDF, including adding signature fields, applying signatures to specific fields, and verifying existing signatures.
rules: Create a PdfFileSignature bound to {doc} (or bind later) and call Sign({int page}, {string_literal reason}, {string_literal signer}, {string_literal location}, {bool visible}, {rect}, {signatureProvider}) to apply a digital signature to a page region.

Use FormEditor on {doc} and call AddField(FieldType.Signature, {string_literal fieldName}, {int page}, {int llx}, {int lly}, {int urx}, {int ury}) to insert a signature placeholder field into the PDF.

To sign an existing signature field, instantiate PdfFileSignature for the target document and invoke Sign({string_literal fieldName}, {string_literal reason}, {string_literal signer}, {string_literal location}, {signatureProvider}).

Verify signatures by creating a PdfFileSignature, binding it to {input_pdf}, then using ContainsSignature() to check for any signatures and VerifySignature({string_literal signatureName}) to validate a specific signature.
warnings: The PKCS1 class is assumed to be Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PKCS1; if the library version changes the namespace may differ.
Rectangle used for signature appearance is System.Drawing.Rectangle, not an Aspose type.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates converting a PDF document to a multi‑page TIFF image using Aspose.Pdf.Facades.PdfConverter, configuring resolution and TIFF settings, and optionally supplying a custom IIndexBitmapConverter implementation for bitmap conversion.
rules: Create a PdfConverter, optionally set its Resolution property, bind the source PDF with BindPdf({input_pdf}), invoke DoConvert(), then call SaveAsTIFF({output_tif}, {int} xResolution, {int} yResolution, {tiff_settings}[, {bitmap_converter}]), and finally Close() to release resources.
Configure TiffSettings before saving, e.g., set Depth = ColorDepth.Format1bpp (or other supported depths) to control the colour depth of the resulting TIFF.

To customize bitmap conversion, implement the IIndexBitmapConverter interface (methods Get1BppImage, Get4BppImage, Get8BppImage) and pass the implementation instance to the SaveAsTIFF overload that accepts an IIndexBitmapConverter.
warnings: The custom WinAPIIndexBitmapConverter relies on Windows GDI functions and will only work on Windows platforms.
Bitmap objects created by the custom converter are not explicitly disposed; callers should ensure proper disposal to avoid memory leaks.

semantic_summary: Shows how to delete the entire file collection (portfolio) embedded in a PDF document and save the resulting PDF without any portfolio items.
rules: Load a PDF using Aspose.Pdf.Document({input_pdf}) and access its Collection property.
Invoke doc.Collection.Delete() to remove all embedded files from the portfolio.
Save the modified document with doc.Save({output_pdf}) to persist the removal.
warnings: If the source PDF does not contain a portfolio, doc.Collection may be null; calling Delete() could raise an exception.
The example overwrites the output file if it already exists.

semantic_summary: Shows how to add an image stamp as a rotated watermark to specific pages of a PDF using the Aspose.Pdf.Facades API.
rules: Create a {doc} from the input PDF, instantiate a PdfFileStamp with that document, configure a Stamp (BindImage, IsBackground, Pages, Rotation, SetOrigin, SetImageSize), then call PdfFileStamp.AddStamp({stamp}) and finally Close() the stamper.

When rotating a Stamp, set its Rotation property (in degrees) and position its origin (SetOrigin) relative to the target page dimensions obtained via PdfFileInfo.GetPageWidth({int}) and GetPageHeight({int}) to achieve the desired placement.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to convert an XML file containing form data into a PDF (or FDF) document using Aspose.Pdf.Facades.FormDataConverter.
rules: Open a read stream for the source XML ({input_xml}) and a write/read stream for the target PDF ({output_pdf}), then invoke FormDataConverter.ConvertXmlToFdf(src, dest) to perform the conversion.
Close both source and destination streams after the conversion to release file handles.
warnings: Method name ConvertXmlToFdf suggests conversion to FDF format, but the example writes the result to a .pdf file; verify the desired output format.
Destination stream must be opened with write access (e.g., FileMode.Create, FileAccess.ReadWrite).

semantic_summary: Class AbsorbedCell in Aspose.Pdf.Text. key methods: CompareTo.
Class AbsorbedRow in Aspose.Pdf.Text. key methods: CompareTo.
Shows how to load a PDF document from an XML definition, retrieve specific page and text segment objects by their IDs, and save the resulting PDF.
rules: To create a PDF from an XML layout, call {doc}.BindXml({xml_path}).
To obtain a PDF element defined in the XML, use {doc}.GetObjectById({object_id}) and cast the result to the expected type (e.g., {page}, {text_fragment}).
After accessing or modifying elements, persist the document with {doc}.Save({output_pdf}).
warnings: The example casts the result of GetObjectById without null checks; IDs must exist in the XML.
No modifications are performed on the retrieved objects; further processing may be required depending on the scenario.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to add invisible (hidden) text to a PDF document, save it, and later retrieve all text fragments—including hidden ones—using a TextFragmentAbsorber.
rules: Create a {doc} (Aspose.Pdf.Document), add a {page} (Aspose.Pdf.Page) via doc.Pages.Add(), then add one or more {text_fragment} (Aspose.Pdf.Text.TextFragment) to page.Paragraphs.
Set hidden text by assigning true to {text_fragment}.TextState.Invisible before adding the fragment to the page.
Persist the hidden text by saving the {doc} to an {output_pdf} file.

To search for text, load the PDF into a new {doc} (Aspose.Pdf.Document) from an {input_pdf} file, instantiate a TextFragmentAbsorber, call absorber.Visit(doc.Pages[<int>]), then iterate absorber.TextFragments to access fragment.Text, fragment.Position, and fragment.TextState.Invisible.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to embed an HTML ordered list (and additional HTML content) into a PDF by creating an HtmlFragment, adding it to a page's paragraph collection, and saving the document.
rules: Create a new Document instance ({doc}) and add a blank Page ({page}) using {doc}.Pages.Add().
Instantiate an HtmlFragment ({html_fragment}) with an HTML string ({string_literal}) and insert it into the page via {page}.Paragraphs.Add({html_fragment}).
Persist the PDF by calling {doc}.Save({output_pdf}).
warnings: The example assumes the Aspose.Pdf namespace is imported and the library is referenced.
HtmlFragment requires well‑formed HTML; malformed markup may cause rendering issues.

semantic_summary: Shows how to insert HTML content into a PDF using the DOM API (HtmlFragment), adjust its margins, and retrieve its rendered rectangle dimensions before saving the document.
rules: Create a {doc} (Aspose.Pdf.Document), add a {page} via {doc}.Pages.Add(), instantiate an {html_fragment} (Aspose.Pdf.HtmlFragment) with a {string_literal} containing HTML markup, optionally set {html_fragment}.Margin.{Top|Bottom} using {int} values, then add the fragment to the page with {page}.Paragraphs.Add({html_fragment}).
After adding an {html_fragment} to a page, its layout information becomes available; you can read {html_fragment}.Rectangle.{Width|Height|...} to obtain the rendered size before saving the {doc}.
warnings: The exact type of the Paragraphs collection is not listed; it is accessed via the Page.Paragraphs property.
Margin property type is inferred as a struct with Top/Bottom fields; exact namespace not confirmed.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to add an HTML fragment to a newly created PDF page using the DOM API, override its default font, set font size and margins, and then save the document.
rules: Create a new {doc} (Aspose.Pdf.Document) and add a {page} (Aspose.Pdf.Page) via doc.Pages.Add().
Instantiate an {html_fragment} (Aspose.Pdf.Text.HtmlFragment) with an HTML {string_literal} and add it to the page using page.Paragraphs.Add({html_fragment}).

Override the default font of the HTML fragment by assigning a new TextState to {html_fragment}.TextState, then set {html_fragment}.TextState.FontSize = {int} and optionally adjust {html_fragment}.Margin.Top and {html_fragment}.Margin.Bottom.
Save the {doc} to an {output_pdf} path using doc.Save({output_pdf}).
warnings: Margin values are in points; setting a large top margin (e.g., 400) may place the content outside the visible page area.
HtmlFragment supports only a subset of HTML/CSS; complex layouts may not render as expected.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to indent all lines after the first line of a text fragment by configuring the SubsequentLinesIndent property of TextFormattingOptions and adding the fragment to a page.
rules: Set {text_fragment}.TextState.FormattingOptions.SubsequentLinesIndent = {int} to apply an indent to every line after the first line of that fragment.
Add the configured {text_fragment} to {page}.Paragraphs before saving the document.
warnings: SubsequentLinesIndent affects only lines that wrap within the same TextFragment; separate TextFragments are treated as separate paragraphs and will not inherit the indent.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates various ways to add and format text in a PDF using Aspose.PDF, including simple text insertion, underlining, borders, custom fonts, paragraphs, hyperlinks, OTF embedding, line breaks, and strike‑out styling.
rules: To add simple text: create {doc}, obtain {page}, instantiate {text_fragment} with {string_literal}, set {text_fragment}.Position = new Position({int}, {int}), optionally configure {text_fragment}.TextState (Font, FontSize, ForegroundColor, BackgroundColor), then use new TextBuilder({page}) and call AppendText({text_fragment}); finally save {doc} to {output_pdf}.
To underline text: set {text_fragment}.TextState.Underline = true before appending with TextBuilder.

To draw a border/polygon around content: instantiate PdfContentEditor, BindPdf({input_pdf}), configure LineInfo (LineWidth={float}, VerticeCoordinate={float}[], Visibility={bool}), then call CreatePolygon(lineInfo, pageNumber, new Rectangle(0,0,0,0), "").
To load a TrueType font from a stream: open FileStream for the font file, assign {text_fragment}.TextState.Font = FontRepository.OpenFont(stream, FontTypes.TTF), then position and append as usual.

To add multi‑line formatted text: create TextParagraph, set Rectangle ({rect}) and FormattingOptions.WrapMode, append one or more TextFragment lines via AppendLine, optionally set SubsequentLinesIndent, then use TextBuilder.AppendParagraph(paragraph).

To embed a hyperlink in a text segment: create TextFragment, add TextSegment objects to its Segments collection, set segment.Hyperlink = new WebHyperlink({string_literal}), and optionally style segment.TextState (ForegroundColor, FontStyle).

To use an OTF font and embed it: load font with FontRepository.OpenFont({string_literal}), assign to {text_fragment}.TextState.Font, then set {text_fragment}.TextState.Font.IsEmbedded = true before appending.
To insert line breaks within a fragment: include Environment.NewLine (or "\n") inside the {string_literal} passed to TextFragment, or place the fragment inside a TextParagraph.
To apply strike‑out and bold styling: set {text_fragment}.TextState.StrikeOut = true and {text_fragment}.TextState.FontStyle = FontStyles.Bold before appending.
warnings: The example uses a helper RunExamples.GetDataDir_AsposePdf_Text() for path resolution which is not defined here.
Some enum values (e.g., TextFormattingOptions.WordWrapMode) are referenced via nested types; ensure correct namespace imports.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to add a text fragment with a colored border, background, and foreground colors to a newly created PDF page using Aspose.PDF.
rules: Create a new {doc} (or load an existing one) and obtain a {page} via {doc}.Pages.Add() or .Pages[<int>].
Instantiate a {text_fragment} with the desired string and set its {text_fragment}.Position to a {position} (x, y) to place it on the page.
Configure visual styling through {text_fragment}.TextState: set Font via FontRepository.FindFont({font}), FontSize ({int}), BackgroundColor ({color}), ForegroundColor ({color}).
To draw a border around the text rectangle, assign a StrokingColor ({color}) to {text_fragment}.TextState.StrokingColor and enable {text_fragment}.TextState.DrawTextRectangleBorder = true.
Add the configured {text_fragment} to the target {page} using a TextBuilder: new TextBuilder({page}).AppendText({text_fragment}).
Save the resulting PDF with {doc}.Save({output_pdf}).

semantic_summary: Shows how to bind an existing PDF, create formatted text, enable word wrap, and add the text to a specific page region using PdfFileMend.
rules: Bind an existing PDF to a PdfFileMend instance: mender.BindPdf({input_pdf});
Create a FormattedText object to specify content and style: new FormattedText({string_literal}, {color}, {color}, {font}, {EncodingType}, {bool}, {int});
Enable word wrap and select a wrap mode before adding text: mender.IsWordWrap = {bool}; mender.WrapMode = {WordWrapMode};
Add the formatted text to a page within a rectangular area: mender.AddText({text_fragment}, {page}, {int x1}, {int y1}, {int x2}, {int y2});
Save the modified PDF and close the mender: mender.Save({output_pdf}); mender.Close();
warnings: Coordinate values are in points; the exact rectangle semantics may differ across PDF versions.
The FormattedText constructor signature may change in future releases; verify parameter order if using a different version.

semantic_summary: The example loads a PDF, searches for the phrase "Lorem ipsum", changes the foreground color of the found text fragment to an axial gradient (red‑to‑blue) using a pattern colorspace, applies underline styling, and saves the modified document.
rules: Use a TextFragmentAbsorber with a {string_literal} to locate text: {doc}.Pages.Accept(absorber); retrieve a specific fragment via {text_fragment} = absorber.TextFragments[{int}]; then modify its TextState properties (e.g., ForegroundColor, Underline) before saving.

To apply a gradient shading as text color, assign a new Color with its PatternColorSpace set to a GradientAxialShading: {text_fragment}.TextState.ForegroundColor = new Color() { PatternColorSpace = new GradientAxialShading({color}, {color}) };
warnings: The example accesses absorber.TextFragments[1] without checking the collection size; this may throw if fewer matches exist.
Gradient shading requires PDF version support; ensure the target PDF version permits pattern colorspaces.

semantic_summary: Shows how to attach a tooltip to specific text in a PDF by locating the text with a TextFragmentAbsorber, creating an invisible ButtonField over the text rectangle, and setting the field's AlternateName property.
rules: Use a TextFragmentAbsorber with a search string ({string_literal}) to locate all matching TextFragment objects in {doc}.
Iterate over the resulting TextFragmentCollection and for each {text_fragment} create a ButtonField using its Page and Rectangle ({page}, {rect}).
Set ButtonField.AlternateName to the desired tooltip text ({string_literal}); viewers display this as a tooltip when hovering over the field.
Add the ButtonField to the document's Form collection (document.Form.Add(field)) to embed the tooltip in the PDF.
warnings: The example relies on the default invisible appearance of ButtonField; if a visible appearance is set, the tooltip may be obscured.
Very long tooltip strings may be truncated or not fully displayed depending on the PDF viewer implementation.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to add semi‑transparent text and a semi‑transparent rectangle to a newly created PDF by configuring alpha channels via Aspose.Pdf.Color methods.
rules: Instantiate a {doc} and add a new {page} using {doc}.Pages.Add().

Create a {text_fragment} with the desired string, set its foreground color to a semi‑transparent color via {color}=Aspose.Pdf.Color.FromArgb(alpha, r, g, b), assign to {text_fragment}.TextState.ForegroundColor, then add the fragment to {page}.Paragraphs.

To draw a semi‑transparent shape, create a {rect}=Aspose.Pdf.Drawing.Rectangle with coordinates, set its GraphInfo.FillColor using Aspose.Pdf.Color.FromRgb(System.Drawing.Color.FromArgb(alpha, baseColor)), add the rectangle to a {graph}=Aspose.Pdf.Drawing.Graph.Shapes, add the {graph} to {page}.Paragraphs, and set {graph}.IsChangePosition = false to keep its position.
warnings: The example uses System.Drawing.Color to embed the alpha channel for FillColor; ensure the target framework supports System.Drawing.
Color.FromRgb expects a System.Drawing.Color; mismatched color spaces may affect the resulting transparency.

semantic_summary: Class CharInfoCollection in Aspose.Pdf.Text. key methods: Remove, GetEnumerator, Clear, CopyTo, Add.
rules: Use CharInfoCollection.Add() to insert items into the collection.

semantic_summary: Enum defining CoordinateOrigin options: BaseLine, Descender.
rules: Use Aspose.Pdf.Text.CoordinateOrigin enum values: BaseLine, Descender.

To create a multi‑column layout, instantiate an Aspose.Pdf.FloatingBox, set its ColumnInfo.ColumnCount = {int}, ColumnInfo.ColumnSpacing = {string_literal} (e.g., "5"), ColumnInfo.ColumnWidths = {string_literal} (space‑separated widths), add desired Paragraph objects (TextFragment, Graph, HtmlFragment, etc.) to box.Paragraphs, then add the box to a page via page.Paragraphs.Add.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to define custom tab stops with specific alignment and leader styles, embed tab placeholders in TextFragment strings, and use them to format columnar text within a PDF page.

Instantiate a {text_fragment} (Aspose.Pdf.Text.TextFragment) with a string containing the placeholder "#$TAB" and pass the previously defined {tab_stops} to its constructor; the placeholder will align text according to the configured tab stops.
Optionally, build a {text_fragment} by adding individual {text_segment} objects (Aspose.Pdf.Text.TextSegment) to its Segments collection, inserting "#$TAB" segments where tab stops are required.
Add each prepared {text_fragment} to {page}.Paragraphs before saving the {doc} to an {output_pdf} file.
warnings: The placeholder "#$TAB" is specific to Aspose.PDF's tab stop processing; using a different placeholder will not trigger tab alignment.
Tab stop positions are absolute coordinates measured in points; they must be chosen to fit within the page width.

semantic_summary: Demonstrates how to use ParagraphAbsorber to extract paragraph and section markup from a PDF page and then draw visual borders (rectangles for sections and polygons for paragraphs) directly onto the page using PDF operators.

To draw a rectangle border around a {rect} on a {page}, add the sequence of operators: GSave, ConcatenateMatrix(1,0,0,1,0,0), SetRGBColorStroke({color}), SetLineWidth({float}), Re(rect.LLX, rect.LLY, rect.Width, rect.Height), ClosePathStroke, GRestore.

To draw a polygon border around a set of {polygon} points on a {page}, add operators: GSave, ConcatenateMatrix(1,0,0,1,0,0), SetRGBColorStroke({color}), SetLineWidth({float}), MoveTo(firstPoint.X, firstPoint.Y), then for each subsequent point add LineTo(point.X, point.Y), finally add LineTo(firstPoint.X, firstPoint.Y), ClosePathStroke, GRestore.

To customize the visual separator line for footnotes on a page, configure a GraphInfo object with properties such as LineWidth, Color, DashArray, and DashPhase, and assign it to Page.NoteLineStyle: {page}.NoteLineStyle = graphInfo.

To change the displayed label of a footnote or endnote, modify the Text property of the associated Note after creation: {text_fragment}.FootNote.Text = "{string_literal}" or {text_fragment}.EndNote.Text = "{string_literal}".

To embed additional content (e.g., images, tables, inline text) inside a footnote, add the content objects to the Note.Paragraphs collection: {text_fragment}.FootNote.Paragraphs.Add(imageOrTableOrFragment).

Alternatively, configure a TextState with Font = {font} and FontSize = {float}, then call TextState.MeasureString({string_literal}) to get the same measurement; the two approaches should yield values within a small tolerance (e.g., Math.Abs(value1 - value2) <= {float}).

Overlay a transparent ButtonField on the target text fragment and assign HideAction to toggle visibility: ButtonField {button} = new ButtonField({page}, {rect}); {button}.Actions.OnEnter = new HideAction({field}, false); {button}.Actions.OnExit = new HideAction({field}); document.Form.Add({button});

Save({output_pdf}) persists the edited PDF; it should be invoked after all edit operations are completed.
